author: @NTC_42
text:
Ummm...

When I was in Junior High School (more than 40 years ago) we took the strips and glued them together; alternating light and dark.

Once that dries, you cut the board into strips again, this time perpendicular to the original cuts. Flip every other strip 180 degrees and glue them together. 

When that all dries, you can square everything up and make the edges pretty.

Now you have a chess board the EASY way.

author: @manemobiili
text: Great job! i made one with really thin pieces so it wouldn't hold together. Use thick squares. Also try to make it large so pieces aren't cramped in.

author: @TerryGilsenan
text:
Rob, it's easier to cut into strips, then glue the strips together and then cut the new stupid board across the strips. Then offset the strips by the width of a block and glue them together again.

Then cut the new bars square.

Its much easier than dealing with 64 separate squares, and you are more likely to get a flat surface without much effort.

author: @meh.7640
text:
wow, this is pathetic ...
what you do is you make strips and glue them together with wood glue in an alternating pattern. then you have a board again. cut that into strips perpendicular to the pattern and glue those together in alternating pattern. it will look a milion times better.

the pathetic part is not that you didn't know. it's that you didn't know but thought you knew and you have to tell others that this is the way although it's just like the next kid would have done it.

author: @arctan2010
text: The chances of accumulating errors increases every time you glue each square. 64 squares is asking for trouble. It is easier to glue 8 long strips in alternating colors. Then you cut it again perpendicular to the length of the alternating colors yielding 8 strips with alternating squares. Then rotate every other strip 180° giving you the checkerboard pattern. Now you only need to glue 8 more strips instead of 64. Glueing 8 strips two times is easier than 64. Also, you didn’t consider the different expansion/contraction rate of different species of wood so I predict you will eventually develop cracks because your squares are fairly thick. I prefer to make the squares only 1/8” thick and glueing the entire board to a stable backer like MDF. I would make the MDF not visible by covering it with wood veneer. The wood glue will overpower thinner squares preventing cracks and MDF does not expand or contract unless you get it wet.

author: @makerslife
text: Great job on the chessboard! Another way you can do it is to cut the strips out. Glue alternating strips together, then turn it 90 degrees and cut out strips again. Then offset and glue your strips. Trim off excess and whoala. Hope this helps Rob!

replies.author: @iverpossehl3316
replies.text: This method saves a lot of work putting together all those "fiddly bits", the individual squares.

author: @adamcrofts58
text: That is a lot of work. It would be easier to glue the long strips together in alternate sequence, black, white, black, white and so on. Then  turn the whole glued strips 90 degrees and cut them again into strips and glue the resulting strips back together again with the white square opposing the black squares then reglue.

author: @RaperJason
text:
Save you a bit of time and effort Rob I made one of these at school and the easy way is to cut the strips then glue them together alternate colours then turn 90 degrees and cut into strips again 
then just turn every other strip the other way round and glue again 
Saves you having to line up lots of little squares and makes the job a lot easier 👍

author: @grendel1960a
text: the slightly easier way is to cut the strips, glue them side by side into a flat board alternating colours, clamp side to side, then when dry run the board through the saw in the other direction and cut strips, now reverse every other strip and glue into the board. saves messing around with all the little pieces
